title: Household first 72 hours command sheet
summary: A practical command sheet for power, water, food, medicine, hygiene, safety, information, and morale during the first three days.
content: The first seventy-two hours should be controlled, boring, and written down. Most households lose time because everyone invents a different priority. The sheet names who checks water, who checks food, who checks medicine, who listens for official updates, who checks vulnerable people, and when the next review happens. It prevents repeated fridge opening, rumor panic, battery waste, forgotten trash, and missed medication. It also gives children and stressed adults a predictable rhythm. The point is not military control; the point is to turn fear into small safe tasks that can be repeated.
materials: Flashlight. Context: “Household first 72 hours command sheet”.
Trash bags. Context: “Household first 72 hours command sheet”.
steps: Write the start time, weather, people present, and visible problem.
Assign roles for water, food, medicine, information, vulnerable people, and safety.
Fill clean containers while water still runs and label doubtful water as untreated.
Keep fridge and freezer closed until a planned food check.
Set morning and evening reviews for water, food, medicine, waste, hygiene, information, and morale.
Use short calm messages and fixed check-in times to protect batteries.
warnings: Do not run fuel-burning equipment indoors or near windows.
Do not drink floodwater or unknown stored water without treatment.
Do not let one exhausted person make every decision.
common_mistakes: Using all battery before knowing the duration.
Ignoring toilets, trash, and vulnerable neighbors until they become urgent.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Household first 72 hours command sheet with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Household first 72 hours command sheet if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Household first 72 hours command sheet and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Household first 72 hours command sheet or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Household first 72 hours command sheet are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.
The routine ignores water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Household first 72 hours command sheet”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Household first 72 hours command sheet as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Household first 72 hours command sheet when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Household first 72 hours command sheet.
short_term: For the first hours of Household first 72 hours command sheet, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Household first 72 hours command sheet remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Household first 72 hours command sheet fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Household first 72 hours command sheet to the real environment: check water, hygiene, food contact, and waste boundaries, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.
